title: Leprosy
authors:
Jan H. Richardus, Hemanta K. Kar,

Zoica Bakirtzief, Wim H. van Brakel

items.title: Understanding Leprosy's Clinical Spectrum
items.content: Leprosy, caused by Mycobacterium leprae, presents a spectrum of disease manifestations from tuberculoid to lepromatous forms. The immune response determines the clinical presentation and severity.
keyPoints: Mycobacterium leprae causes leprosy.
Clinical manifestations include skin, peripheral nervous system, eyes, and upper respiratory tract involvement.
Disease spectrum ranges from tuberculoid to lepromatous leprosy.
Peripheral nerve predilection can lead to disability and social stigma.

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Treatment in Leprosy
items.content: Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for curing leprosy and preventing irreversible impairments. Despite being a neglected disease, new cases continue to be reported globally.
keyPoints: 174,087 new cases were reported from 182 countries in 2022.
Lack of awareness delays diagnosis and treatment.
Early intervention can cure the disease and prevent chronic complications.

items.title: Characteristics of M. leprae Morphology
items.content: M. leprae is a rod-shaped, acid-fast bacterium that primarily infects macrophages and Schwann cells. It appears irregularly stained or granular when dead, while brightly stained bacilli are viable. The morphologic index measures the percentage of uniformly stained solid bacilli in slit-skin smears.
keyPoints: Morphology: 1–8 μm length, 0.3 μm diameter
Staining: Acid-fast, irregularly stained when dead
Viable Bacilli: Brightly and uniformly stained
Morphologic Index: Percentage of viable bacilli in slit-skin smear

items.title: Bacteriologic Index and Disease Spectrum
items.content: The bacteriologic index quantifies the density of M. leprae bacilli on a logarithmic scale from 0 to 6+, reflecting disease severity from tuberculoid to lepromatous forms. It decreases by an average of 1 log unit per year with multidrug therapy.
keyPoints: Bacteriologic Index: Logarithmic scale, 0 to 6+
Disease Spectrum: Tuberculoid to lepromatous
Therapy Impact: Decreases by 1 log unit/year with treatment

items.title: Genomic and Culture Characteristics of M. leprae
items.content: M. leprae's genome is circular, with a molecular mass of $2.2 	imes 10^9$ Da, 3,268,203 base pairs, and 57.8% guanine-plus-cytosine content. Its reductive evolution explains the inability to culture it in artificial media; instead, propagation occurs in animal models like armadillos and mice.
keyPoints: Genome: Circular, $2.2 	imes 10^9$ Da
Base Pairs: 3,268,203
GC Content: 57.8%
Culture Challenges: Reductive evolution, not culturable in vitro

items.title: Mycobacterium lepromatosis and its Clinical Relevance
items.content: M. lepromatosis, isolated in 2008 from patients with diffuse lepromatous leprosy, is similar to M. leprae both microbiologically and clinically. Both species are acid-fast, noncultivable, and preferentially infect skin and peripheral nerves. Clinically distinguishing between the two is not necessary as they respond similarly to antimycobacterial treatments.
keyPoints: M. lepromatosis isolated in 2008 from patients with diffuse leprosy of Lucio and Latapi
Found mainly in Mexico and Central America
Similar to M. leprae: acid-fast, noncultivable, infects skin and peripheral nerves
Both respond well to the same antimycobacterial regimens

items.title: Global Trends in Leprosy Case Detection
items.content: The global trend in new leprosy case detection has shown a significant decline since 2000, with a peak around the year 2000. The downward trend accelerated between 2001 and 2005 due to reduced leprosy control activities after WHO's declaration of elimination as a public health problem. Since 2016, cases have continued to decrease, suggesting potential elimination in the next 25 years.
keyPoints: 174,087 new cases reported globally in 2022
Trend peaked around 2000, declined between 2001-2005
WHO declared leprosy eliminated as a public health problem in 2000 (prevalence <1 per 10,000)
New case detection below 200,000 since 2016

items.title: WHO Leprosy Disability Grading System
items.content: The WHO leprosy disability grading system evaluates disabilities in the eyes, hands, and feet. Grade 0 indicates no impairment or problems; grade 1 signifies presence without severe effects; grade 2 denotes visible impairment or severe visual issues (vision score worse than 6/60). The Eye-Hand-Foot (EHF) score aggregates these assessments to indicate overall impairment status.
keyPoints: WHO disability grading: eyes, hands, feet
Grade 0: no anesthesia/impairment; Grade 1: presence without severe effects; Grade 2: visible impairment/severe visual issues
Vision score worse than 6/60 indicates grade 2 for eyes
5% of patients have a grade 2 disability

items.title: Transmission Dynamics of M. leprae
items.content: Understanding the transmission of M. leprae is challenging due to its long incubation period, inability to culture the organism, and diagnostic difficulties in early disease stages. The main route of entry is assumed to be respiratory, but skin transmission through wounds or tattoos is also possible.
keyPoints: Transmission understanding is limited due to long incubation period (2 to ≥10 years).
M. leprae can be shed from the mouth and nose in untreated multibacillary cases.
Respiratory tract is the main assumed route of entry, but skin transmission is possible through wounds or tattoos.

items.title: Incubation Period and Risk Factors for Leprosy
items.content: The incubation period for leprosy ranges from 2 to ≥10 years, with multibacillary cases having a longer duration (5 to ≥15 years) compared to paucibacillary cases (≈2–5 years). Key risk factors include close and prolonged contact with multibacillary patients, along with genetic susceptibility.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2 to ≥10 years overall; 5 to ≥15 years for multibacillary leprosy.
Paucibacillary leprosy incubation period: ≈2–5 years.
Risk factors include intimacy and duration of contact with multibacillary patients, genetic susceptibility.

items.title: Role of Immunologic Resistance in Leprosy Pathogenesis
items.content: The pathogenic process of leprosy begins with M. leprae entering peripheral nerves and being engulfed by Schwann cells. The type of leprosy that develops is determined by the host's immunologic resistance, particularly the cell-mediated immune response to the bacillus.
keyPoints: Immunologic resistance varies across the Ridley-Jopling spectrum: strong in tuberculoid (TT) and weak in lepromatous (LL).
Granuloma formation depends on immunity: macrophage-lymphocyte interaction with immunity, macrophages only without.
Tuberculoid granulomas cause nerve destruction leading to anesthesia and muscle weakness.

items.title: Granuloma Formation in Leprosy
items.content: The formation of a granuloma is central to the pathogenesis of leprosy, occurring through macrophage-lymphocyte interaction when there is immunity or by macrophages alone otherwise.
keyPoints: In tuberculoid leprosy, strong immune response leads to fixed epithelioid cells and giant cell formation.
Borderline leprosy shows less focal cellular response with fewer bacilli and nerve damage.
Lepromatous leprosy involves macrophage granulomas with more bacilli and widespread dissemination.

items.title: WHO Simplified Clinical Classification of Leprosy
items.content: The WHO simplified classification system categorizes leprosy based on clinical criteria due to the lack of reliable slit-skin smear facilities in many settings.
keyPoints: Paucibacillary leprosy: one to five skin lesions and no or only one involved peripheral nerve.
Multibacillary leprosy: six or more skin lesions and/or more than one involved peripheral nerve.

items.title: Indeterminate Leprosy: Early Clinical Presentation
items.content: Indeterminate Leprosy (IL) is an early form of leprosy characterized by one or a few hypopigmented or faintly erythematous macular lesions. These lesions are typically 1–5 cm in diameter and occur on the external aspects of limbs, buttocks, and face. IL often presents with mild to moderate impairment of touch and/or thermal sensations without nerve thickening.
keyPoints: Lesions measure 1–5 cm in diameter
Occur on external aspects of limbs, buttocks, and face
Mild to moderate impairment of touch/thermal sensation
No thickening of cutaneous/peripheral nerves

items.title: Tuberculoid Leprosy: Characteristic Lesions and Sensory Loss
items.content: Tuberculoid (TT) leprosy presents with well-defined hypopigmented macules or raised erythematous/brown plaques. These lesions have a clear-cut edge that often slopes inward, creating an annular configuration. Complete loss of fine touch and temperature sensations occurs over the lesion surface due to nerve destruction. Larger cutaneous nerves may be thickened in some cases.
keyPoints: Lesions are well-defined macules or raised plaques
Complete loss of fine touch/temperature sensation
Lesions seldom measure >10 cm in diameter
Lepromin skin test is strongly positive

items.title: Borderline Tuberculoid Leprosy: Transition and Reaction Risk
items.content: Borderline Tuberculoid (BT) leprosy features three to nine or more macular or plaque-type lesions with variable sizes and contours. Lesions may have poorly defined margins, sometimes blending into normal skin. Sensory loss is less intense than in TT leprosy. BT leprosy is prone to type 1 leprosy reactions (T1R), which can exacerbate lesions and nerve damage if untreated.
keyPoints: Three to nine or more lesions
Variable sizes/contours with poorly defined margins
Less intense sensory loss than TT
Susceptibility to type 1 leprosy reactions (T1R)

items.title: Borderline Lepromatous Leprosy: Symmetrical Lesions and Sensory Changes
items.content: In borderline lepromatous (BL) leprosy, patients present with numerous bilateral macular lesions that are erythematous or hypopigmented, typically 2-3 cm in diameter. These lesions may have a coppery hue and become symmetrical over time. Sensory loss is noted particularly over older lesions but not fresh ones.
keyPoints: Numerous bilateral, round or oval macular lesions
Lesions are erythematous or hypopigmented with moderately defined borders
Diameter of lesions: 2-3 cm
Coppery hue may be present
Symmetrical distribution over time
Sensory loss over older lesions, not fresh ones
Lepromin test is negative

items.title: Lepromatous Leprosy: Systemic Involvement and Facial Disfigurement
items.content: In lepromatous (LL) leprosy, systemic involvement is extensive with AFB found in multiple organs. Characteristic facial disfigurements include 'lion face' due to coarse induration and loss of eyebrows and earlobes.
keyPoints: AFB present in lymph nodes, spleen, liver, bone marrow, adrenal glands, muscles, tooth pulp, testes, oral cavity, nose, larynx, eyes
Eye involvement: corneal anesthesia, episcleritis, iridocyclitis, iris atrophy, cataract and glaucoma, lagophthalmos, ulceration, blindness
'Lion face' appearance due to facial induration, eyebrow loss, earlobe thickening
Nasal involvement: crusting, epistaxis, chronic rhinitis, saddle-nose disfigurement

items.title: Histoid Leprosy: Rare Form with High Bacteriologic Indices
items.content: Histoid leprosy is a rare form of LL leprosy characterized by waxy, shiny nodules and plaques. Histologic examination reveals spindle-cell granulomas, and slit-skin smear shows high bacteriologic indices without globi.
keyPoints: Waxy, shiny, firm nodules and plaques
Symmetrical or asymmetrical distribution over normal-looking skin
Histologic examination: spindle-cell granulomas
High bacteriologic and microbiologic indices without globi

items.title: Type 1 Leprosy Reaction (T1R) - Reversal Reaction
items.content: T1R is a delayed hypersensitivity reaction characterized by sudden changes in cell-mediated immunity, leading to acute swelling and redness of skin lesions. It involves infiltration by CD4+ T lymphocytes, particularly T helper cells, and can cause painful neuritis with potential nerve damage and disfigurement.
keyPoints: T1R is a delayed hypersensitivity reaction associated with sudden alteration in CMI status.
Involves infiltration of lesions by activated CD4+ T lymphocytes, especially T helper cells.
Characterized by acute swelling and redness of skin lesions.
Nerves may be painful and tender due to neuritis, leading to nerve damage and disfigurement.
Silent neuritis can cause sensory and motor impairment without obvious inflammation signs.

items.title: Type 2 Leprosy Reaction (T2R) - Erythema Nodosum Leprosum (ENL)
items.content: T2R, or ENL, is an immune complex-mediated syndrome causing inflammation of the skin, nerves, and other organs. It presents with evanescent, pink-to-red lesions that change color over time and may be accompanied by constitutional symptoms like fever and malaise.
keyPoints: T2R (ENL) is a type III hypersensitivity reaction involving immune complexes.
Causes inflammation of skin, nerves, and other organs with general malaise.
Lesions are evanescent, pink-to-red, maculopapular, papular, nodular, or plaque-like.
Constitutional symptoms include fever and malaise, with possible painful joint swelling.
Severe T2R may lead to swollen, tender nerve trunks with sensory and motor deficits.

items.title: Lucio's Phenomenon - Vasculitis in Leprosy
items.content: Lucio's phenomenon is a variant of erythema nodosum necroticans observed in diffuse leprosy, characterized by marked vasculitis and thrombosis leading to skin hemorrhage and infarction. It presents as bluish-red plaques that may develop into purplish lesions with central hemorrhagic infarcts.
keyPoints: Observed in diffuse leprosy of Lucio and Latapi, a variant of erythema nodosum necroticans.
Characterized by marked vasculitis and thrombosis causing skin hemorrhage and infarction.
Clinically presents as bluish-red plaques with an erythematous halo.
Lesions become purplish at the center, potentially leading to blister formation and necrotic eschar.
Patients remain afebrile throughout the course of Lucio's phenomenon.

items.title: Cardinal Signs for Leprosy Diagnosis
items.content: Leprosy can be diagnosed when at least one of three cardinal signs is present: hypopigmented or erythematous skin lesions with sensory loss, peripheral nerve involvement with thickening and sensory impairment, or positive AFB detection.
keyPoints: Hypopigmented/erythematous skin lesion(s) with definite loss/impaired sensation
Peripheral nerves thickened with sensory impairment; palpation of specific nerves like ulnar, radial, etc.
Positive AFB in slit-skin smears or biopsy PCR

items.title: Diagnostic Tools for Leprosy
items.content: Testing of skin sensation and specific diagnostic tools are crucial for leprosy diagnosis. Light-touch, pain, and thermal sensations are tested using cotton, a toothpick, or computer-assisted equipment.
keyPoints: Light-touch sensation with cotton/wool/feather
Pain assessed by sharp/blunt ends of a wooden/bamboo toothpick
Thermal sensation thresholds with computer-assisted testing

items.title: Role of PGL-1 Antibody Test in Leprosy Diagnosis
items.content: The PGL-1 antibody test is a serologic tool used to diagnose leprosy. It detects antibodies against M. leprae with high sensitivity, especially useful in multibacillary cases.
keyPoints: PGL-1 ELISA positive in 90–95% of multibacillary and 25–60% of paucibacillary cases
ML flow test positive in 92–97% of multibacillary and 32–40% of paucibacillary patients

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Leprosy
items.content: Leprosy must be differentiated from various skin conditions and neuropathies due to its late diagnosis, which increases the risk of nerve damage. Differential diagnoses include hypopigmented macules such as pityriasis alba, vitiligo, and sarcoidosis, among others. For plaques and nodular lesions, consider granuloma annulare and cutaneous T-cell lymphomas. ENL lesions should be distinguished from erythema nodosum and nodular vasculitis.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes pityriasis alba, vitiligo, sarcoidosis, cutaneous leishmaniasis, lupus miliaris disseminatus faciei, nodular histiocytosis, lupus erythematosus, mycosis fungoides, and secondary syphilis.
For ENL lesions, consider erythema nodosum, nodular vasculitis, and cutaneous polyarteritis nodosa.
Mononeuropathy differential includes diabetes, amyloidosis, and myxedema; acute onset polyneuropathy considerations include Guillain-Barré syndrome and toxic polyneuropathy.

items.title: Touch Sensation Testing in Leprosy
items.content: The Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test is the most reliable for assessing touch sensation in leprosy, focusing on ulnar, median, and posterior tibial nerves. In resource-limited settings, a ballpoint pen can substitute for filaments, using a similar testing protocol. If nerve impairment is recent (<6 months) or newly diagnosed, glucocorticoid treatment should be initiated.
keyPoints: Test ulnar, median, and posterior tibial nerves for touch sensation.
Use Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test; substitute with ballpoint pen if necessary.
Initiate glucocorticoid treatment for impairment <6 months' duration or new diagnosis.

items.title: Ultrasound Testing of Nerves in Leprosy
items.content: Palpable enlargement of peripheral nerves is a key sign of leprosy. Ultrasound imaging can accurately detect nerve enlargement, even with portable equipment, aiding diagnosis and indicating when anti-inflammatory treatment may be needed.
keyPoints: Palpable nerve enlargement is a cardinal sign of leprosy.
Ultrasound detects nerve enlargement accurately, supporting diagnosis.
Indicates onset of neuropathy warranting anti-inflammatory treatment.

items.title: WHO Recommended Multidrug Therapy Regimen for Leprosy
items.content: The WHO recommends a specific multidrug regimen for the treatment of leprosy, differing between multibacillary and paucibacillary forms. For multibacillary leprosy, the regimen includes monthly rifampin, daily dapsone, and both daily and monthly clofazimine over 12 months. Paucibacillary leprosy is treated with monthly rifampin and daily dapsone for 6 months.
keyPoints: Multidrug therapy consists of rifampin, dapsone, and clofazimine.
Multibacillary treatment: 12 months; Paucibacillary treatment: 6 months.
WHO does not recommend a shortened regimen due to potential increased risk of relapse.

items.title: Adverse Events and Monitoring in Leprosy Treatment
items.content: Rifampin, dapsone, and clofazimine each have specific adverse events. Rifampin may cause mild transient hepatic aminotransferase elevation but is generally safe at recommended doses. Dapsone can lead to hemolysis, anemia, or severe reactions like DDS syndrome, necessitating careful monitoring. Clofazimine causes skin discoloration and ichthyosis, which are significant for patient compliance.
keyPoints: Rifampin: Rare mild hepatic aminotransferase elevation; no need to discontinue.
Dapsone: Risk of hemolysis, anemia, DDS syndrome with 10% fatality rate.
Clofazimine: Causes skin discoloration and ichthyosis.

items.title: WHO's Stance on Treatment Duration for Multibacillary Leprosy
items.content: The WHO advises against a shortened treatment duration of three drugs over six months for multibacillary leprosy due to limited and inconclusive evidence, with concerns about increased relapse risk. The standard regimen remains 12 months.
keyPoints: WHO does not recommend a 6-month regimen for multibacillary leprosy.
Concerns include potential increase in relapse rates.

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Relapse in Leprosy
items.content: Relapse in multibacillary leprosy is defined by an increase of at least 2+ in the bacteriologic index, often accompanied by clinical deterioration. It occurs in less than 1% of patients except those with a high initial bacillary load (bacteriologic index ≥ 4). Relapses typically occur within five years post-treatment and can be managed with re-administration of the same multidrug regimen.
keyPoints: Relapse defined as an increase of at least 2+ in bacteriologic index
Occurs in <1% of patients, except those with initial bacteriologic index ≥ 4
Relapses typically occur within five years post-treatment
Re-administration of the same multidrug regimen is effective for relapse

items.title: Approach to Rifampin Resistance in Leprosy
items.content: Rifampin resistance, though rare, necessitates alternative treatment regimens. WHO guidelines recommend using at least two second-line drugs—clarithromycin, minocycline, or a quinolone (ofloxacin, levofloxacin, or moxifloxacin)—plus clofazimine for 6 months, followed by clofazimine plus one of the second-line drugs daily for an additional 18 months. For resistance to both rifampin and ofloxacin, a regimen including clarithromycin, minocycline, and clofazimine is advised.
keyPoints: Rifampin resistance requires alternative treatment regimens
WHO recommends two second-line drugs plus clofazimine for 6 months
Followed by clofazimine plus one second-line drug daily for an additional 18 months
For dual resistance, use clarithromycin, minocycline, and clofazimine

items.title: Optimal Management of Type 1 Leprosy Reactions with Glucocorticoids
items.content: For treating Type 1 leprosy reactions, oral short-acting glucocorticoids like prednisolone are preferred. The initial dose is typically 1 mg/kg/day, up to a maximum of 60–80 mg, though the WHO recommends starting at 40 mg in endemic regions. Treatment should be tapered slowly over 20 weeks by reducing 5 mg every two weeks for better outcomes and reduced relapse rates.
keyPoints: Initial prednisolone dose: 1 mg/kg/day up to 60–80 mg, WHO recommends 40 mg
Tapering schedule: reduce by 5 mg every 2 weeks over 20 weeks
Clinical response should guide treatment adjustments
Examine patients bi-weekly with nerve function assessment

items.title: Effective Management of Type 2 Leprosy Reactions (ENL) Using Thalidomide and Alternatives
items.content: Mild first-time Type 2 reactions can be treated with aspirin and pentoxifylline. For rapid suppression, thalidomide is effective but teratogenic, requiring caution in women of childbearing age. It's administered at 100–200 mg once or twice daily, tapered after 1-2 weeks for acute episodes. In severe cases, a maintenance dose of 50 mg may be used. Alternatives include high-dose glucocorticoids and clofazimine, with the latter causing skin discoloration.
keyPoints: Thalidomide dose: 100–200 mg once or twice daily
Caution in women of childbearing age due to teratogenicity
Taper thalidomide after 1-2 weeks for acute episodes
Clofazimine regimen: 300 mg/day for 1 month, then 200 mg/day for 1 month, followed by 100 mg maintenance
Side effects of clofazimine include skin discoloration and gastrointestinal issues

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.title: Optimal Timing and Efficacy of Glucocorticoid Treatment for Leprosy-Induced Nerve Function Impairment
items.content: Glucocorticoids, specifically high-dose prednisolone, are effective in treating nerve function impairment due to leprosy when initiated shortly after symptom onset. The prognosis for full recovery is favorable if treatment starts early, with some recovery possible up to 6 months post-onset.
keyPoints: High-dose prednisolone is the drug of choice for nerve function impairment in leprosy.
Early initiation of glucocorticoid treatment improves prognosis for full recovery.
Some recovery can be expected up to 6 months after onset, but diminishes with each new episode.
Nerve function impairment persisting >6 months does not benefit from glucocorticoid treatment.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.title: Management of Neuropathic Pain in Leprosy Patients
items.content: Neuropathic pain is common in leprosy and can be managed with a combination of medications, including tricyclic antidepressants, phenothiazines, anticonvulsants (such as carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, gabapentin, pregabalin), analgesics, and anti-inflammatory drugs.
keyPoints: Neuropathic pain is common in leprosy patients.
Treatment options include tricyclic antidepressants, phenothiazines, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, gabapentin, pregabalin).
Medication can be combined with analgesics and anti-inflammatory drugs based on patient needs.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.title: Importance of Supervised Multidrug Therapy in Leprosy
items.content: Supervised multidrug therapy is crucial for ensuring adherence and effective treatment outcomes in leprosy. Regular administration, particularly the supervised 4-weekly dose of rifampin and clofazimine, helps maintain consistent drug intake.
keyPoints: Supervised 4-weekly doses of rifampin and clofazimine are essential.
Flexible arrangements can facilitate adherence, such as allowing patients to take home multiple blister packs for travel or seasonal labor under supervision.

items.title: Nerve Function Assessment in Leprosy Patients
items.content: Regular nerve function assessments are vital during leprosy treatment to detect and manage insidious nerve damage. This is particularly important for patients with multibacillary disease, who have a high risk of additional nerve damage.
keyPoints: Monthly nerve function assessment is recommended at each clinic visit during multidrug therapy.
Patients with multibacillary disease have up to a 65% risk of additional nerve damage.
Multibacillary patients without initial impairment and paucibacillary patients with impairment have a 16% chance of developing new damage.
Paucibacillary patients without initial impairment are at lowest risk (3%) and may only need assessments at the start and end of therapy.

items.title: Health Education for Leprosy Patients
items.content: Effective health education is critical to ensure treatment adherence and address potential social and mental health issues. Educating patients at diagnosis and post-treatment completion helps manage expectations about cure and sequelae.
keyPoints: Education should cover the curable nature of leprosy, drug intake discomforts, interruption of transmission, and importance of adherence.
Post-treatment education must differentiate between bacterial cure and disease sequelae like nerve impairment or reactions.
Patients need to understand that they may require ongoing care for posttreatment complications such as claw hand or neuropathic foot.

items.title: Post-Treatment Counseling and Follow-Up in Leprosy
items.content: After completing multidrug therapy for leprosy, patients should receive counseling to address misconceptions about contagion, skin changes, and nerve function. They must be informed that while they are no longer contagious, some skin discoloration may persist due to clofazimine, and nerve impairment might continue to improve but is not guaranteed. Patients need to return for follow-up if new symptoms arise.
keyPoints: Patients are no longer contagious after treatment completion.
Hypopigmentation in skin lesions may take a long time to resolve.
Clofazimine-induced skin discoloration will gradually disappear over months.
Nerve impairment improvement post-treatment is uncertain.
Return for clinic visit if new skin signs or fresh nerve damage occurs.
Check-up every 6 months for at least 3 years for patients with preexisting nerve function impairment, multibacillary infection, or those who experienced a reaction during therapy.

items.title: Managing Leprosy Reactions Post-Treatment
items.content: Patients should be educated that new skin signs or nerve damage post-treatment are often due to leprosy reactions, not a recurrence of the disease. These situations require careful management from both medical and social perspectives to address patient concerns about 'leprosy coming back.' Regular follow-ups are crucial for those at risk of further episodes.
keyPoints: New skin signs or nerve damage post-treatment often indicate leprosy reactions, not recurrence.
Management should consider both medical and social aspects due to potential misinterpretation as disease relapse.
Patients with preexisting conditions or who had reactional episodes during therapy need biannual check-ups for 3 years.

items.title: Physical Rehabilitation in Leprosy
items.content: Leprosy patients often require physical rehabilitation due to peripheral neuropathy and its disabling consequences. This includes reconstructive surgery for paralysis of facial, ulnar, median, or posterior tibial nerves, with pre- and postoperative physical therapy being crucial. Physical therapy is also indicated when muscles are not completely paralyzed or contractures are too stiff for surgery.
keyPoints: Reconstructive surgery may be needed for nerve paralysis (facial, ulnar, median, posterior tibial).
Pre- and postoperative physical therapy is crucial.
Physical therapy indicated when muscles not fully paralyzed or contractures too stiff for surgery.

items.title: ISSO Routine for Self-Care in Leprosy
items.content: The ISSO (inspection, soaking, scraping, and oiling) routine is a well-tested self-care method for managing hands and feet affected by leprosy. It involves inspecting limbs for hotspots, soaking in water for 15 minutes, scraping excess calluses, and applying non-fragrant oil to maintain skin condition.
keyPoints: ISSO stands for inspection, soaking, scraping, and oiling.
Soaking duration: 15 minutes.
Routine helps keep skin supple despite sensory and autonomic damage.

items.title: Mental and Social Support for Leprosy Patients
items.content: Leprosy patients face mental health challenges due to stigma, social exclusion, and the burden of chronic disease management. Therapeutic group meetings and self-care groups can improve mental well-being by reducing depression, encouraging self-acceptance, and promoting confidence.
keyPoints: Mental health issues include depression, anxiety, and suicidal thoughts.
Social stigmatization is a major trigger for mental suffering.
Group meetings and self-care groups help ameliorate depression and promote confidence.

items.title: Role of Vaccines in Leprosy Prevention
items.content: Vaccination strategies, including the BCG vaccine and new candidates like MIP and LepVax, are pivotal for postexposure prophylaxis against leprosy.
keyPoints: BCG vaccine provides varying degrees of protection against leprosy and is used as postexposure immunoprophylaxis in Brazil.
Two promising vaccines under development: MIP (India) based on killed Mycobacterium indicus pranii, and LepVax (University of Washington).
Trials for new vaccines are in early stages; proof of efficacy will take years.

items.title: Innovations in Postexposure Chemoprophylaxis for Leprosy
items.content: Post-exposure chemoprophylaxis (PEP) is a significant advancement for preventing leprosy among close contacts of patients.
keyPoints: PEP targets household and other close contacts of leprosy patients to prevent transmission.
Introduction of PEP represents an important innovation in leprosy control strategies.

items.question: Which pathogen is responsible for causing leprosy?
items.explanation: Leprosy is caused by Mycobacterium leprae.

items.question: What are the primary clinical manifestations of leprosy?
options: Skin, peripheral nervous system, eyes, and upper respiratory tract
items.explanation: Leprosy primarily affects the skin, peripheral nervous system, eyes, and upper respiratory tract.

items.question: What is a significant consequence of immunologically mediated reactional states in leprosy?
items.explanation: Immunologically mediated reactional states can cause nerve damage, often resulting in disability.

items.question: Which temperature range is required for the survival and proliferation of M. leprae?
items.explanation: M. leprae requires a temperature range of 27°C to 30°C for survival and proliferation.

items.question: In which cells does M. leprae predominantly multiply in an unrestricted fashion at the lepromatous end of the disease spectrum?
items.explanation: M. leprae multiplies in macrophages (lepra cells) at the lepromatous end of the disease spectrum.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of M. leprae's cell wall composition?
items.explanation: M. leprae's cell wall is composed of peptidoglycans bound to branched-chain polysaccharides.

items.question: Which lipid component in M. leprae's capsule is antigenically specific for the bacterium?
items.explanation: The trisaccharide in phenolic glycolipid-1 (PGL-1) is antigenically specific for M. leprae.

items.question: Which mycobacterial species was isolated from patients with diffuse lepromatous leprosy in 2008?
items.explanation: In 2008, M. lepromatosis was isolated from patients with diffuse lepromatous leprosy.

items.question: Which regions are primarily affected by the clinical variety of leprosy caused by M. lepromatosis?
items.explanation: M. lepromatosis is found mainly in Mexico and Central America.

items.question: What factor significantly contributed to the decline in new case detection of leprosy after 2000?
items.explanation: The decline was due to reduced leprosy control efforts after WHO declared it eliminated as a public health problem.

items.question: What is the registered prevalence of leprosy defined as?
options: The number of new cases detected annually
The number of patients receiving treatment at a point in time
The total number of people infected with M. leprae
The percentage of population affected by leprosy
items.explanation: Registered prevalence is the number of patients receiving treatment at a specific time.

items.question: Which of the following is considered a main reservoir for M. leprae infection?
items.explanation: Humans are assumed to be the main reservoir of infection for M. leprae.

items.question: What is the presumed primary route of entry for M. leprae into the body?
items.explanation: The main route of entry into the body for M. leprae is assumed to be the respiratory tract.

items.question: Which type of leprosy case represents an important reservoir for undetected and untreated cases in the community?
items.explanation: The higher incidence rate among household contacts of multibacillary cases suggests they are an important reservoir for undetected and untreated cases.

items.question: Which type of immune response primarily determines the histopathologic changes in nerve and skin for leprosy?
items.explanation: The immunologic resistance, particularly the cell-mediated immune (CMI) response to M. leprae and its antigens, determines the histopathologic changes in nerve and skin.

items.question: In which type of leprosy is the immunologic resistance weakest?
items.explanation: Immunologic resistance is weakest in lepromatous leprosy.

items.question: What type of cells are primarily involved in forming granulomas when there is immunity to M. leprae?
items.explanation: Granulomas are formed by macrophage-lymphocyte interaction when there is immunity.

items.question: In which leprosy type do bacilli multiply within Schwann cells and perineural cells?
items.explanation: In lepromatous (LL) leprosy, bacilli multiply within Schwann cells and perineural cells.

items.question: Which peripheral nerve is commonly involved in leprosy affecting the lower limbs?
items.explanation: The lateral popliteal and posterior tibial nerves are mentioned as being involved in the lower limbs.

items.question: In Tuberculoid (TT) leprosy, what is typically observed on slit-skin smear examination?
options: Moderate number of AFB are found
items.explanation: In TT leprosy, no acid-fast bacilli (AFB) are normally found on slit-skin smear examination.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of Borderline Tuberculoid (BT) leprosy lesions?
options: Edges of plaque lesions slope inward
items.explanation: A striking feature of BT leprosy is susceptibility to a type 1 leprosy reaction (T1R).

items.question: Which cranial nerves are commonly involved in leprosy?
items.explanation: The trigeminal and facial nerves are commonly involved in leprosy.

items.question: Which leprosy type is characterized by numerous bilateral, round or oval, macular lesions with moderately defined borders and may have a coppery hue?
items.explanation: Borderline Lepromatous (BL) leprosy presents with numerous bilateral, round or oval, macular lesions that may have a coppery hue.

items.question: In which form of leprosy is the bacteriologic index from slit-skin smear examination typically 4+ to 6+ with globi?
items.explanation: In Lepromatous (LL) leprosy, the bacteriologic index from slit-skin smear examination is typically 4+ to 6+ with globi.

items.question: Which symptom is associated with advanced LL leprosy involving the nose?
items.explanation: Advanced LL leprosy can lead to saddle-nose disfigurement due to septal perforation from bony destruction.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of histoid leprosy?
options: Presence of globi in slit-skin smear
items.explanation: Histoid leprosy is characterized by waxy, shiny, firm nodules and plaques on normal-looking skin.

items.question: Which type of leprosy reaction is characterized by a delayed hypersensitivity reaction involving CD4+ T lymphocytes?
items.explanation: Type 1 Leprosy Reaction (T1R) is marked by infiltration of lesions by activated CD4+ T lymphocytes.

items.question: What type of hypersensitivity reaction does Type 2 Leprosy Reaction (T2R) represent?
items.explanation: Type 2 Leprosy Reaction (T2R) is an example of a type III hypersensitivity reaction.

items.question: Which leprosy reaction may present with 'silent neuritis' leading to sensory and motor impairment without obvious signs?
options: Both Type 1 and Type 2 Leprosy Reactions
items.explanation: 'Silent neuritis' is more common during Type 1 Leprosy Reaction.

items.question: Which leprosy reaction can lead to the formation of nerve abscesses in its severe form?
items.explanation: In severe Type 1 Leprosy Reaction, nerve abscesses may be formed.

items.question: Which nerve is NOT commonly palpated in a leprosy patient?
items.explanation: The sciatic nerve is not listed as one of the commonly palpated nerves in leprosy patients.

items.question: What is the purpose of performing a slit-skin smear in leprosy diagnosis?
options: To determine the presence of AFB
items.explanation: Slit-skin smears are used to establish the presence of acid-fast bacilli (AFB) in leprosy.

items.question: Which test is specifically used to measure cellular immunity against M. leprae?
items.explanation: The Lepromin test measures cellular immunity against M. leprae.

items.question: Which antigen is NOT used in PCR methods for detecting M. leprae?
items.explanation: The HIV p24 antigen is not used in PCR methods for detecting M. leprae.

items.question: Which condition must be differentiated from the hypopigmented macules of leprosy?
items.explanation: Hypopigmented macules of leprosy must be differentiated from conditions like pityriasis alba.

items.question: Which nerve is NOT typically tested for touch sensation in leprosy?
items.explanation: The ulnar, median, and posterior tibial nerves are typically tested for touch sensation in leprosy.

items.question: What is the most reliable test for assessing touch sensation impairment in leprosy?
items.explanation: The Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test is the most reliable for assessing touch sensation impairment.

items.question: Which motor function is NOT typically affected in leprosy?
items.explanation: Leprosy commonly affects eye closure, finger abduction, and thumb opposition, but not biceps flexion.

items.question: Which drug is a keystone of WHO recommended multidrug therapy for multibacillary leprosy?
items.explanation: Rifampin is a keystone drug in WHO recommended multidrug therapy for multibacillary leprosy.

items.question: What is the treatment duration for paucibacillary leprosy according to WHO guidelines?
items.explanation: The treatment duration for paucibacillary leprosy is 6 months.

items.question: Which adverse event associated with dapsone can lead to a fatality rate of 10%?
items.explanation: The DDS syndrome is a severe adverse event associated with dapsone that has a fatality rate of 10%.

items.question: Which drug causes skin discoloration ranging from red to purple or black as an adverse effect?
items.explanation: Clofazimine causes skin discoloration ranging from red to purple or black.

items.question: What is the typical timeframe within which relapses occur after completing multidrug therapy for multibacillary leprosy?
items.explanation: Relapses usually occur <5 years after the end of multidrug therapy.

items.question: How can a therapeutic trial with glucocorticoids help differentiate between T1R and relapse in paucibacillary leprosy?
options: Lack of response favors T1R diagnosis
Definite improvement within 4 weeks indicates T1R
No change with glucocorticoids indicates a new infection
items.explanation: A definite improvement within 4 weeks of initiation of glucocorticoid therapy indicates T1R.

items.question: According to WHO guidelines, what is the recommended treatment for rifampin-resistant leprosy?
options: Daily treatment with at least two second-line drugs plus clofazimine for 6 months
Treatment with only clarithromycin and minocycline for 24 months
Use of first-line drugs exclusively for 12 months
No specific regimen; use any available antibiotics
items.explanation: WHO recommends daily treatment with at least two second-line drugs plus clofazimine for 6 months.

items.question: What is the initial recommended dose of prednisolone for treating Type 1 Reactions (T1R) according to WHO guidelines?
items.explanation: The WHO recommends an initial dose of 40 mg of prednisolone for T1R.

items.question: Which drug is noted for its rapid suppression of clinical signs in Type 2 Reactions (ENL) but is restricted due to teratogenicity?
items.explanation: Thalidomide rapidly suppresses clinical signs in ENL but is restricted due to its teratogenicity.

items.question: What is the usual initial daily dose of clofazimine for preventing recurrent Type 2 Reactions (ENL)?
items.explanation: The usual initial daily dose of clofazimine is 300 mg for preventing recurrent ENL.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.question: Which medication is considered the drug of choice for treating episodes of sensory or motor nerve function impairment without skin signs in leprosy?
items.explanation: High-dose prednisolone is the drug of choice for treating nerve function impairment without skin signs.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.question: What is the likelihood of recovery from nerve function impairment if glucocorticoid treatment is started shortly after onset?
options: Poor, with no chance of full recovery
Good, with a high chance of full recovery
Diminished significantly over time regardless of treatment
Only possible if combined with nerve decompression surgery
items.explanation: If glucocorticoid treatment is started shortly after onset, the prognosis for full recovery is good.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.question: Which of the following statements about nerve function impairment lasting more than 6 months is true?
options: It benefits significantly from glucocorticoid treatment.
Recovery can be expected up to 12 months after onset.
The likelihood of recovery diminishes with every new episode.
Nerve decompression surgery is highly effective.
items.explanation: Generally, nerve function impairment that has persisted for >6 months does not benefit from glucocorticoid treatment.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.question: Which healthcare professional is primarily responsible for leprosy treatment in middle- and high-income countries?
items.explanation: In middle- and high-income countries, the main treatment responsibility usually falls to a dermatologist.

items.question: What is the highest risk percentage of additional nerve damage in patients with multibacillary leprosy who already have nerve damage at the start of treatment?
items.explanation: Patients with multibacillary disease, who already have nerve damage at the start of treatment, have a risk as high as 65% for additional nerve damage.

items.question: What is an important aspect of health education when communicating the diagnosis of leprosy to patients?
options: Explaining that cure means absence of symptoms
Emphasizing the curability and interruption of disease transmission through drug intake
Discussing only the possible discomforts of drug intake
Focusing on the need for reconstructive surgery
items.explanation: The physician must explain that the disease is caused by a curable microbial infection and cover the interruption of disease transmission through drug intake.

items.question: What type of therapy requires regular visits to ensure treatment adherence in leprosy management?
items.explanation: Regular visits are important for supervised multidrug therapy, especially the supervised 4-weekly dose of rifampin and clofazimine.

items.question: What should patients be reassured of regarding their contagiousness after completing multidrug therapy for leprosy?
options: They remain contagious but to a lesser degree.
Their contagiousness depends on the presence of skin discoloration.
They need additional treatment to become non-contagious.
items.explanation: Patients should be reassured that they are no longer contagious after completing multidrug therapy.

items.question: What is the expected outcome for skin discoloration due to clofazimine after treatment completion?
options: It will gradually disappear in the following months.
It requires additional medication to resolve.
items.explanation: Skin discoloration due to clofazimine is expected to gradually disappear in the following months after treatment completion.

items.question: When should patients at risk of further episodes of reaction or nerve function impairment return for a check-up?
options: Every month for the first year.
Every 6 months for at least 3 years.
items.explanation: Patients at risk should return for a check-up every 6 months for at least 3 years after being released from treatment.

items.question: Which self-care routine for hands and feet is evidence-based and consists of inspection, soaking, scraping, and oiling?
items.explanation: The ISSO routine involves inspecting the affected limbs, soaking them in water, scraping excess calluses, and applying oil.

items.question: What is a key principle of teaching patients with leprosy to manage their disabilities?
items.explanation: The key principle is teaching patients and former patients to self-manage their disabilities.

items.question: What type of footwear should be worn if sensation on the soles of the feet is impaired due to leprosy?
options: Protective footwear with a strong sole and soft insole
items.explanation: Protective footwear with a strong sole and soft insole is recommended to distribute pressure.

items.question: What mental health issues are common among leprosy patients due to social exclusion?
items.explanation: Common mental health comorbidities include depression, anxiety, and suicidal thoughts.

items.question: Which traditional method was primarily used in the control of leprosy before novel strategies were developed?
options: Early case detection and multidrug treatment
items.explanation: Leprosy control was traditionally based on early case detection and multidrug treatment.

items.question: What has been the role of the BCG vaccine in leprosy prevention?
options: It is used as a primary preventive measure for all individuals.
It provides varying degrees of protection and is used as postexposure immunoprophylaxis in Brazil.
It completely prevents transmission of M. leprae.
It has been replaced by the synthetic LepVax vaccine.
items.explanation: The BCG vaccine provides varying degrees of protection against leprosy and is used as postexposure immunoprophylaxis for contacts of leprosy patients in Brazil.

items.question: Which statement best describes the current status of novel vaccines for leprosy?
options: They are widely used and have proven efficacy.
Trials are in early stages, and sufficient proof of efficacy will take years.
They have replaced BCG as the primary vaccine for leprosy prevention.
They provide complete protection against M. leprae.
items.explanation: Trials for novel vaccines like MIP and LepVax are in early stages, and sufficient proof of efficacy will take years.

items.statement: Leprosy is no longer considered a neglected disease.
items.explanation: Leprosy is still considered a neglected disease with many unaware of its existence.

items.statement: Early diagnosis and treatment can prevent most chronic complications in leprosy.
items.explanation: Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to curing the disease and preventing chronic complications.

items.statement: M. leprae can be cultured in artificial media.
items.explanation: M. leprae has never been grown in artificial media due to its reductive evolution and gene decay.

items.statement: The genome of M. leprae is circular with an estimated molecular mass of $2.2 	imes 10^9$ Da.
items.explanation: The genome of M. leprae is indeed circular and has the specified molecular mass.

items.statement: The WHO defined leprosy elimination as having less than 1 case per 10,000 population globally.
items.explanation: Elimination was defined by the WHO as a prevalence of <1 case per 10,000 population.

items.statement: Leprosy is most commonly diagnosed in children under 5 years old.
items.explanation: Leprosy is rare among children <5 years of age.

items.statement: Transmission of M. leprae from humans to monkeys is well-documented.
items.explanation: There is no evidence of transmission to humans through contact with infected monkeys.

items.statement: The incubation period for multibacillary leprosy is generally shorter than that for paucibacillary leprosy.
items.explanation: The incubation period for multibacillary leprosy appears to be longer than that for paucibacillary leprosy.

items.statement: The formation of a granuloma in response to M. leprae is always preceded by infiltration by both macrophages and lymphocytes.
items.explanation: Granuloma formation is preceded by infiltration by lymphocytes alone, as seen in indeterminate leprosy.

items.statement: In borderline lepromatous (BL) leprosy, there is significant nerve damage.
items.explanation: In BL leprosy, there are macrophage granulomas with lymphocytes but little nerve damage.

items.statement: Indeterminate Leprosy (IL) always progresses to a determinate form of the disease.
items.explanation: IL may either heal spontaneously or progress to a determinate form, depending on CMI status.

items.statement: In Mid-Borderline (BB) leprosy, the lepromin test is positive.
items.explanation: The lepromin test in BB leprosy is negative.

items.statement: In LL leprosy, the lepromin test gives positive results.
items.explanation: The lepromin test is negative in all degrees of lepromatous leprosy.

items.statement: Primary neuritic leprosy presents with skin lesions.
items.explanation: Primary neuritic leprosy involves only peripheral nerve involvement without skin lesions.

items.statement: Lucio's phenomenon is characterized by fever and malaise.
items.explanation: Patients with Lucio's phenomenon remain afebrile throughout.

items.statement: Type 2 Leprosy Reaction (T2R) is also known as erythema nodosum leprosum.
items.explanation: Type 2 Leprosy Reaction (T2R) is indeed referred to as ENL (erythema nodosum leprosum).

items.statement: A positive result for AFB in slit-skin smears can be one of the cardinal signs indicating a diagnosis of leprosy.
items.explanation: AFB presence in slit-skin smears is one of the three cardinal signs used to diagnose leprosy.

items.statement: The Lepromin test gives positive results in patients with multibacillary leprosy.
items.explanation: A negative lepromin test is generally seen in multibacillary leprosy, indicating a lack of protective cellular response.

items.statement: Ultrasound imaging can detect nerve enlargement in leprosy.
items.explanation: Ultrasound imaging is used to accurately detect nerve enlargement in leprosy.

items.statement: Heat detection thresholds are often affected after touch sensation impairment in leprosy.
items.explanation: Heat detection thresholds are often affected several months before touch sensation is impaired.

items.statement: The WHO recommends a uniform regimen of three drugs for 6 months for multibacillary leprosy.
items.explanation: WHO does not recommend a shortened treatment duration of 6 months for multibacillary leprosy due to limited and inconclusive evidence.

items.statement: Rifampin causes induction of hepatic cytochrome p450 at the recommended dosage.
items.explanation: A monthly dose of rifampin does not cause induction of hepatic cytochrome p450.

items.statement: Relapse rates in multibacillary leprosy are well below 1% except among patients with a very high bacillary load at the start of treatment.
items.explanation: Relapse rates are indeed well below 1%, except for those with a high initial bacillary load.

items.statement: Patients with rifampin-resistant leprosy can be treated using the same first-line multidrug regimen.
items.explanation: Rifampin resistance requires treatment with second-line drugs as per WHO guidelines.

items.statement: Type 2 Reactions (ENL) often resolve in approximately 2 weeks.
items.explanation: T2R tends to be transient, often resolving in ~2 weeks.

items.statement: Clofazimine is preferred over thalidomide for all patients with Type 2 Reactions due to fewer side effects.
items.explanation: While clofazimine can prevent recurrent ENL, it has significant adverse gastrointestinal effects and causes skin discoloration.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.statement: Evidence from randomized controlled trials supports the use of nerve decompression surgery for silent neuropathy in leprosy.
items.explanation: Evidence from randomized controlled trials is lacking for nerve decompression surgery.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.statement: Tricyclic antidepressants are one of the classes of medication available for treating neuropathic pain in leprosy.
items.explanation: Tricyclic antidepressants are indeed used for treating neuropathic pain.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.statement: Patients with paucibacillary disease who do not have nerve function impairment at diagnosis are at the highest risk for developing new nerve damage.
items.explanation: They are at the lowest risk, with a 3% chance of developing nerve damage.

items.statement: Leprosy reactions and new nerve damage can occur up to 3 years after completion of multidrug treatment.
items.explanation: These manifestations can indeed occur up to 3 years post-therapy.

items.statement: Nerve impairment is guaranteed to improve after release from leprosy treatment.
items.explanation: Nerve impairment may continue to improve, but this is not certain.

items.statement: Patients should be instructed to return if new skin signs or fresh nerve damage occurs.
items.explanation: Patients must return for evaluation if any new skin signs or fresh nerve damage occurs.

items.statement: Leprosy is the most notorious of all stigmatized health conditions.
items.explanation: Leprosy is noted as probably the most notorious of all stigmatized health conditions.

items.statement: Joint interventions addressing health-related stigmas for multiple conditions are not strategically or financially attractive.
items.explanation: Joint interventions are described as strategically and financially attractive.

items.statement: The introduction of post-exposure chemoprophylaxis (PEP) is considered an important innovation for leprosy prevention.
items.explanation: Postexposure chemoprophylaxis for household and other close contacts of leprosy patients is an important innovation.

items.statement: The MIP vaccine has been proven effective and is currently in use.
items.explanation: Trials for the MIP vaccine are still in early stages, and sufficient proof of efficacy will take years.

items.assertion: M. leprae infects mainly macrophages and Schwann cells.
items.reason: The bacterium's preference for cooler temperatures explains its tropism for these cell types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why M. leprae infects macrophages and Schwann cells.

items.assertion: The bacteriologic index is a measure of viable bacilli among total bacilli.
items.reason: It is calculated as the percentage of uniformly stained solid bacilli on slit-skin smear examination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how the bacteriologic index is measured.

items.assertion: M. lepromatosis and M. leprae are clinically indistinguishable in individual patients.
items.reason: Both organisms respond well to the same antimycobacterial regimens.
items.explanation: Differentiation is not necessary as both respond to similar treatments.

items.assertion: The global trend in new case detection of leprosy has been steadily increasing since 1990.
items.reason: There was a dramatic decline between 2001 and 2005 due to reduced control activities.
items.explanation: The trend was static until 2001, then declined dramatically; it did not increase.

items.assertion: The tuberculoid (TT) type of leprosy is characterized by strong immune responses.
items.reason: Strong immune responses in TT leprosy lead to the formation of granulomas with fixed epithelioid cells and giant cells, causing nerve destruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The WHO simplified classification system for leprosy is based on clinical criteria only.
items.reason: Slit-skin smear facilities are not available or dependable in many countries, necessitating reliance on clinical criteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: In Tuberculoid (TT) leprosy, the skin lesions are characterized by complete loss of fine touch and temperature sensations.
items.reason: This is due to destruction of underlying superficial cutaneous nerves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: In Borderline Tuberculoid (BT) leprosy, lesions tend to downgrade across the spectrum to LL leprosy.
items.reason: This is due to an increasing bacteriologic index and a regressed CMI response causing nerve damage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: In LLp disease, peripheral nerve involvement occurs late and is bilateral and symmetrical.
items.reason: Peripheral nerve damage in LLp leprosy arises due to the advanced stage of the disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Histoid leprosy is characterized by a low bacteriologic index without globi.
items.reason: Histoid leprosy shows specific spindle-cell granulomas on histologic examination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: Silent neuritis can lead to sensory and motor impairment.
items.reason: Silent neuritis is defined as progressive sensory or motor impairment in the absence of symptoms such as pain, paresthesia, or tenderness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Type 1 Leprosy Reaction is associated with immune complex-mediated syndrome.
items.reason: Type 1 Leprosy Reaction involves a delayed hypersensitivity reaction rather than an immune complex-mediated process.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Type 1 Leprosy Reaction is not immune complex-mediated; it's a delayed hypersensitivity reaction.

items.assertion: The PGL-1 antibody test yields positive results in the majority of multibacillary leprosy cases.
items.reason: PGL-1 is a specific lipid on the M. leprae cell wall, and its detection indicates infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PGL-1 antibody test yields positive results in multibacillary cases.

items.assertion: Gene amplification techniques like PCR can detect M. leprae even when smear-negative.
items.reason: PCR methods are sensitive to as few as 1–10 bacilli and can amplify specific gene stretches of M. leprae.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PCR is effective in smear-negative cases.

items.assertion: Glucocorticoid treatment should be given if nerve function impairment in leprosy is of <6 months' duration.
items.reason: Early changes detected by nerve conduction tests do not show improved outcomes with glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why glucocorticoid treatment should be given.

items.assertion: Laser Doppler measurement is a sensitive method for detecting peripheral autonomic nerve damage.
items.reason: This technique can replace the Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test in field conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as Laser Doppler measures autonomic function, not touch sensation.

items.assertion: Clofazimine can accumulate in active leprosy skin lesions.
items.reason: This accumulation makes the lesions more prominent and causes noticeable skin discoloration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Rifampin is given daily in WHO-recommended multidrug therapy regimens.
items.reason: This frequent administration helps prevent adverse effects recognized from its use in tuberculosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because rifampin is given only monthly, not daily.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN
items.assertion: The prognosis for full recovery from nerve function impairment is good if treatment starts shortly after onset.
items.reason: Nerve decompression surgery has been proven effective in randomized controlled trials.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false as evidence from randomized controlled trials for nerve decompression surgery is lacking.
items.reference: Leprosy · TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS OF NERVE FUNCTION IMPAIRMENT | TREATMENT OF (NEUROPATHIC) PAIN

items.assertion: Patients with multibacillary leprosy are at highest risk for additional nerve damage.
items.reason: They already have nerve damage at the start of treatment.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: Supervised Multidrug Therapy does not require regular visits for treatment adherence.
items.reason: Regular visits help ensure patients complete their treatment and adhere to the therapy schedule.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; supervised multidrug therapy does require regular visits.

items.assertion: Patients should be counseled that leprosy is no longer contagious after treatment.
items.reason: This reassurance helps prevent misunderstanding and stigma associated with the disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients should be reassured of their non-contagious status.

items.assertion: Therapeutic group meetings can ameliorate depression among leprosy patients.
items.reason: These meetings focus on prevention of disabilities and mental well-being.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Leprosy is given high priority on the public health agenda in endemic countries.
items.reason: It features common characteristics of neglected tropical diseases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, despite leprosy having characteristics of NTDs.
